subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Clear, wellâstated aim with realâworld educational relevance
Comprehensive description of schema theory and link to Anderson & Pichert
Explicit, operationalized hypotheses with correctly identified IV and DV
weaknesses: Minor unfinished sentence in theory section indicates incomplete articulation
Operationalisation could be more precise (e.g., detailed scoring criteria)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three introduction strands comfortably sit in the 5â6 band. The aim is clearly stated and its relevance explained, schema theory is described with an explicit link to the investigation, and variables are operationalized. Minor language repetition, an incomplete sentence and slightly imprecise operational definitions prevent a perfect 6, so a 5 is justified.

strengths: Research design is not only described but justified with reference to demand characteristics
Sampling technique is explained and participant demographics are provided
Relevant controlled variables and materials are identified, including a wellâdesigned distraction task
weaknesses: Rationale for certain materials (e.g., duration of distraction task) lacks depth
Some control justifications stop midâidea and need completion
Reliance on selfâreported memory skill introduces potential bias without mitigation
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The exploration strands achieve the 3â4 band. Design and sampling are fully explained, and controls are described. Though materials are named and some rationale given, justifications are uneven and occasionally incomplete. This warrants the full 4 marks for a strong but not flawless exploration section.

strengths: Appropriate use of means, SDs and MannâWhitney U for small nonânormal samples
Accurate interpretation of statistical outcomes in relation to the hypothesis
Descriptive statistics are clearly presented in a table
weaknesses: Bar chart lacks error bars and has a poorly chosen yâaxis scale
Graph units and sample sizes (n) are not clearly indicated
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Statistical analysis and interpretation meet topâband requirements with accurate application and clear linkage to hypothesis, but minor reporting issues keep this at 5â6 bandâs lower edge. Graphical presentation contains errors that drop it to the middle band. Averaging these aspects yields a solid 4 out of 6 for analysis.

strengths: Findings are discussed in relation to schema theory, showing theoretical linkage
Strengths and limitations are relevant and explained with clear ties to the investigation
Modifications are linked to identified limitations
weaknesses: Depth of critical engagement is limited in discussing findings
Justification for some modifications is brief or speculative
Discussion could more explicitly map each proposed change back to specific limitations
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Evaluation strands sit in the 3â4 band: findings reference theory but lack depth, strengths/limitations are relevant and explained, and modifications are linked but not fully justified. Overall performance warrants 3 out of 6, reflecting moderate evaluation quality.

strandSelections.reasoning: A bar chart summarises the mean recall for each condition and addresses the hypothesis.  Nevertheless, the vertical axis scale is poorly chosen and the figure lacks error bars and clear units, indicating minor errors; thus the work satisfies the middle band with 3 marks.

The student states the statistical outcome, clearly links it to retention of the null hypothesis, and explains what this means for the research hypothesis and schema theory.  Interpretation is coherent and grounded in the data, warranting 5 marks in the top band.

Both null and research hypotheses state the IV (perspective: gardener only vs. gardener then parent) and DV (number of correctly recalled items in second recall).  The variables are operationalised (specific perspectives, second recall count).  Terminology is accurate; therefore the work meets the top-band descriptors, meriting 5 rather than 6 because operationalisation could be crisper (e.g., precise scoring criteria).

Opportunity sampling is identified and the rationale (shared schemas, similar academic background) is explained.  Participant demographics are provided, fulfilling the âexplainedâ requirement, so 4 marks are appropriate.

Schema theory is outlined (reconstructive memory, top-down processing) and explicitly tied to Anderson & Pichert as well as to the present replication.  The mechanism (schema-congruent recall) is connected to the investigation aim.  Minor inaccuracies (e.g., repetition of âreconstructiveâ) prevent a perfect 6, but the description and link are both present and mostly accurate.

Findings are discussed in relation to schema theory and possible reasons (schema overlap) are considered, meeting the descriptor for description with reference to theory.  Depth and critical engagement are limited, so the lower mark of the 3-4 band is allocated.

The student clearly states an aim: to see whether switching from a gardener to a parent schema affects second-recall performance, and then explains why this matters for bilingual high-school students and their learning strategies.  The link to real-world educational relevance shows explanation rather than mere mention, justifying placement in the top band, though some claims about grade improvement are speculative, so the strand is awarded 5 not 6.

Several relevant strengths (independent design, shared background) and limitations (participant variability, timing, instruction clarity) are identified and explained, showing clear relevance to the investigation.  The breadth and explanation justify 4 marks within the 3-4 band.

Materials (story, jokes, recall sheets) and several controlled variables (language, timing, distraction task) are named and some reasoning is given.  However, depth is uneven and not every control is fully justified, placing the work at the lower end of the 3-4 band (score 3).

Proposed modifications (matched-pairs design, shorten response time, review instructions, screen for gardening knowledge) are linked to stated limitations, but justification is brief and sometimes speculative.  This matches the requirements for the 3-4 band, earning 3 marks.

The independent-samples design is not only described but justified with reference to demand characteristics, demonstrating explanation rather than description.  Consequently the strand sits in the 3-4 band and earns the upper mark, 4.

Means, standard deviations and an appropriate non-parametric test (Mann-Whitney U) are used correctly for small, non-normal independent samples.  Calculations and p-value are correctly interpreted; therefore application is accurate.  Minor reporting issues (e.g., odd labelling of P1) keep the score at 5 not 6.

annotations.comment: The research aim clearly states the IV (schema condition) and DV (number of correctly recalled items), operationalizing both variables effectively.
The null hypothesis defines IV and DV but could be strengthened by specifying the scoring criteria for âcorrectly recalled items.â
The introduction provides a clear, coherent explanation of schema theory, including reconstructive memory and topâdown processing, demonstrating strong theoretical grounding.
The repeated use of âreconstructiveâ suggests redundancy; consider varying terminology to maintain reader engagement and clarity.
The summary of Anderson & Pichert (1977) is accurate but lacks an explicit link to how your replication will adapt their paradigm beyond simply changing content.
This paragraph ends mid-sentence, indicating an incomplete idea. Ensure all theoretical connections are fully articulated.
The rationale for replicating this study in bilingual high-school students is well articulated, linking schema theory to educational strategies.
The description of opportunity sampling is included but the rationale explanation stops mid-idea; complete the reasoning on similarity of memory skills.
Participant demographics (age, gender) are clearly reported; however, discuss how this opportunistic class sample affects generalizability.
The distraction task using jokes is well designed to reduce rehearsal, showing thoughtful control of confounding variables.
Materials and controlled variables are listed but the rationale for why 2 minutes of joke ranking suffices as a distractor needs further justification.
Session time and language controls are appropriate, but reliance on self-reported memory skill is subjective and may introduce bias.
Table 1 succinctly presents means and standard deviations for each condition, demonstrating clear descriptive statistics.
The bar chart addresses the hypothesis but lacks error bars and an appropriate y-axis scale, reducing its informational value.
The Mann-Whitney U test is appropriate, but clarify why a one-tailed test was chosen and confirm the critical value aligns with this directionality.
The evaluation effectively situates findings within schema theory, demonstrating good linkage between results and background model.
The discussion of schema choice variability is insightful, correctly identifying how individual schema familiarity may affect recall.
The suggestion to switch to a matched-pairs design is valid, but expand on exactly how memory skill matching would reduce variability.
The research hypothesis is precise and directional, but adding an expected effect size or magnitude would improve predictive clarity.
The independent-samples design is not only described but justified in terms of demand characteristics, which demonstrates clear research design explanation.
The narrative description of the table omits sample sizes (n) and should include them to support the statistical summary.
The sample strength discussion demonstrates awareness of shared cultural schemas, reinforcing sampling relevance.
The proposal to use a volunteer sample of gardening-interested students is plausible but should explain how this specifically mitigates identified bias.
Identifying excessive recall time as a limitation is appropriate; strengthen by suggesting optimal timing based on pilot data.
Several thoughtful modifications are suggested, but could you explicitly map each proposed change back to the limitation it addresses?
The reference list includes relevant sources but inconsistent APA formatting (italics, indent) should be corrected for academic rigor.
